title: practical english usage - Part 323
content:
to be a cousin of my mother’s.                      -» One day I happened to get talking to a woman on a train, and she turned out If you happen to see Imogen, ask her to phone me.

happens unexpectedly or by chance.

Happen can be used with a following infinitive to suggest that something 479 happen        to   ...

For half in clock times (e.g. half past two), ►    325.

For more information about numbers and counting expressions, ►    322.

I’ ve been waiting for an hour and a half not I’ve been waiting for one and a half hours, (     ... one and a haff hour?) The  expression one and a half goes with a plural noun. Compare: 5 one   and     a  half or Could I have half a pound of grapes? (   ... a half pound...) expressions of measurement.

Ha/f usually comes before the article ɑ/ɑn,  but it is possible to put it after in 4 half     a  and   a  half I didn’t like the second half of the film.

Would you like the big half or the small half?

used in this case.

We   use the before half if we  are saying which  half we  mean. Before a noun, of is 3 the    half not I’ ve bought some chocolate. You can have half, (     .. . the haff) Half can be used without a following noun, if the meaning is clear.

2 no   following       noun 'Did you like the books? 'I’ ve only read half of them.’ We   use half of before pronouns.

The train was half an hour late. (AmE  also ... a half hour...) not I just need half a loaf of bread, (     ... haff of a loaf...) not I live half a mile from here, (     ... haff of a mile...) Of  is not used in expressions of measurement and quantity.

Half (of) my friends live abroad, (     Haff of my friends lives ...) When  half (of) is followed by a plural noun, the verb is plural.

I gave him  half (of) a cheese pie to keep him  quiet.

She spends half (of) her time travelling, (     She spends a/the half...) in this case.

possessive or demonstrative). We   do not normally put a or the before half We   use half or half of mostly before a noun with a determiner (article, half     (of) 1 478 hə/f        _________ Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  480  hardly, scarcely and no sooner other communication, we  generally use hear. Compare: understanding the whole of a performance, speech, piece of music, broadcast or going on, in progress. To  talk about the result of listening: experiencing or Listen (to) is mostly used to talk about concentrating on experiences that are 3  complete       experiences:        hear I didn't hear the phone because I was listening to the radio.

'Listen carefully, please.' 'Could you speak louder? I can't hear you very well.' / heard them talking upstairs, but I didn't really listen to their conversation.

something deliberately. Compare: possible. You can hear something without wanting to, but you can only listen to progress. It emphasises the idea of concentrating, trying to hear as well as Listen (to) is used to talk about paying attention to sounds that are going on, in 2  listen      (to): meaning Can you hear me?

Suddenly I heard a strange noise. (     Suddenly I listened to a strange noise.) Hear is the ordinary word to say that something ‘ comes to our ears’ .

1  hear:     meaning 481  hear     and   listen      (to) No sooner had she agreed to marry him  than she started to have doubts.

Hardly had I closed my eyes when I began to imagine fantastic shapes.

In a formal or literary style, inverted word order is possible (►  270.7).

We no sooner sat down in the train than I felt sick.

I had no sooner closed the door than somebody knocked.

She was hardly/scarcely inside the house before the kids started screaming.

I had hardly/scarcely closed my eyes when the phone rang.

... no sooner... than ...

... scarcely. .. when/before...

... hardly ... when/before...

to suggest that one thing happened very soon after another. Note the These three expressions can be used (often with a past perfect tense, ►    53) 480 hardly,       scarcely         and   no   sooner       ______ I'll take my swimming things, in case I should happen to find a pool open.
